issues.message: The figure uses a bar chart with a single bar to present one scalar metric (val_bpb). For a single-condition result, a text table or inline numeric reporting is often clearer; if comparison is intended (e.g., baseline vs QAT+roundtrip), the comparative condition is missing from the plot.

Error bars are computed but the confidence interval values are identical to the mean (ci_low = ci_high = value), so the error bars have zero extent. This may confuse reviewers by suggesting uncertainty information that is not actually present.

The x-axis tick label 'metrics' is not very informative for a figure meant to compare training regimes. A more descriptive category name (e.g., 'Float baseline', 'QAT+roundtrip') would better communicate the experimental condition.

The figure title and caption describe both absolute runtime and a runtime ratio vs. baseline with a dashed 1.10× threshold and a quick_gate_passed indicator, but the plotted data only show a single bar for runtime values and symmetric zero-width error bars. There is no explicit baseline, no ratio axis or ratio values, no dashed reference line at 1.10×, and no quick-gate indicator. The chart type (single bar) is also under-informative for a runtime comparison.

No legend is provided. Given the caption mentions multiple semantic elements (baseline vs. candidate, runtime ratio, quick_gate_passed indicator, threshold line), a legend or clear in-plot annotations would be needed to distinguish them.

The y-axis grid is enabled with alpha=0.3, which is generally acceptable, but combined with white bar edges and potentially light background (depending on fallback style), some journals prefer slightly lighter grid lines or no grid for single-bar plots to maintain a cleaner look.

The y-axis label states 'Runtime (ms) and runtime ratio (× baseline)' but the plotted data correspond only to a single set of runtime values. There is no second axis or explicit ratio scale, which can confuse readers.

The figure uses a bar chart with a single category and no visible confidence interval (ci_low == ci_high == value). For a single metric this could be more cleanly represented as a point (dot) with an error bar or omitted CI, unless visual consistency with other bar charts in the paper is required.

With only one positive value (3.3919) and no variation, the y-axis scale and visual context might overemphasize an arbitrary absolute level. Consider showing delta around zero (e.g., include y=0 reference line or symmetric limits) to better convey 'change vs. baseline', especially if negative is an improvement as described in the caption.
